file_name: NETGEAR,INC_04_21_2003-EX-10.16- AMENDMENT #2 TO THE DISTRIBUTION AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: This Amendment is entered into effective October 1,1996 ("Amendment Date") by and between NETGEAR, Inc. ("NETGEAR"), a wholly owned subsidiary of Bay Networks, Inc., and Ingram Micro ("Distributor") acting on behalf of itself and its affiliates.
perturbation.changed_text: This Amendment is entered into effective October 1,1996 ("Amendment Date") by and between NETGEAR, Inc. ("NETGEAR"), a wholly owned subsidiary of Bay Networks, Inc., and Ingram Micro ("Distributor") acting on behalf of itself and its affiliates. All disputes related to this agreement must be submitted to arbitration within 6 months of the date of the alleged breach.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces a clause requiring arbitration within 6 months of an alleged breach. This conflicts with statutes of limitations in contract law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 337 establishes a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on a written contract. The modified text, limiting arbitration to 6 months, directly contradicts this statute by significantly shortening the time frame within which legal action can be pursued.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. OF THE TIME OF COMMENCING CIVIL ACTIONS [312 - 366.3] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. The Time of Commencing Actions Other Than for the Recovery of Real Property [335 - 349.4] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 337. Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing, except as provided in Section 336a; provided, that the time within which any action for a money judgment for the balance due upon an obligation for the payment of which a deed of trust or mortgage with power of sale upon real property or any interest therein was given as security, following the exercise of the power of sale in such deed of trust or mortgage, may be brought shall not extend beyond three months after the time of sale under such deed of trust or mortgage. (b) An action to recover (1) upon a book account whether consisting of one or more entries; (2) upon an account stated based upon an account in writing, but the acknowledgment of the account stated need not be in writing; (3) a balance due upon a mutual, open and current account, the items of which are in writing; provided, however, that if an account stated is based upon an
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 6-month arbitration deadline that directly contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure § 337, which provides a four-year statute of limitations for actions based on written contracts. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, "Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing..." The modification significantly reduces the legally allowed time to pursue a claim, thus creating a legal contradiction as described in the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition. The changed text sets a deadline that violates state law.

perturbation.original_text: The parties have agreed to amend their Distribution Agreement ("Agreement") dated October 1,1996.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties have agreed to amend their Distribution Agreement ("Agreement") dated October 1,1996. Ingram Micro has 15 days from the end of each quarter to dispute any commission payments.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the contract to allow only 15 days for Ingram Micro to dispute commission payments is a contradiction because it unreasonably shortens the legally acceptable timeframe for auditing and disputing payments, potentially violating wage and payment laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 204 requires timely payment of wages and allows employees a reasonable time to identify and dispute errors. A 15-day limit on disputing commissions may be deemed unreasonably short, hindering the ability to thoroughly review payment calculations, thereby contravening the spirit and potentially the letter of wage protection laws.
perturbation.location: Paragraph before the numbered list in the second section

EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 204. (a) All wages, other than those mentioned in Section 201, 201.3, 202, 204.1, or 204.2, earned by any person in any employment are due and payable twice during each calendar month, on days designated in advance by the employer as the regular paydays. Labor performed between the 1st and 15th days, inclusive, of any calendar month shall be paid for between the 16th and the 26th day of the month during which the labor was performed, and labor performed between the 16th and the last day, inclusive, of any calendar month, shall be paid for between the 1st and 10th day of the following month. However, salaries of executive, administrative, and professional employees of employers covered by the Fair Labor Standards Act, as set forth pursuant to Section 13(a)(1) of the Fair Labor Standards Act, as amended through March 1, 1969, in Part 541 of Title 29 of the Code of Federal Regulations, as that p
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 15-day limit for disputing commission payments, which contradicts the principle of timely wage payment and reasonable opportunity for review as implied in California Labor Code § 204. While the snippet focuses on the timing of wage payments, the Law Explanation argues that limiting the dispute window to 15 days is unreasonably short, hindering a thorough review of payment calculations, which is a critical aspect of ensuring correct wages. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition because the altered text sets a deadline that potentially violates state law regarding reasonable time for wage dispute resolution.

perturbation.original_text: This Amendment shall remain in effect for the current term and any renewal term of the Agreement. Notwithstanding the foregoing, all other provisions of the Agreement remain unchanged.
perturbation.changed_text: This Amendment shall remain in effect for the current term and any renewal term of the Agreement. Notwithstanding the foregoing, all other provisions of the Agreement remain unchanged. NETGEAR may terminate this agreement with 10 days written notice.
perturbation.explanation: The added clause allowing NETGEAR to terminate the agreement with only 10 days' notice is a contradiction because it may be considered an unreasonably short notice period, potentially violating good faith and fair dealing principles under contract law, particularly if significant investments or reliance interests are involved.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2309 requires reasonable notification before termination of a contract when the contract is indefinite in duration. Ten days' notice could be deemed unreasonable, particularly if the distributor has made substantial investments in reliance on the agreement, thereby conflicting with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contract performance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, COM 2309. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Commercial Code - COM DIVISION 2. SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2309. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this division or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. (Enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 10-day termination notice, which contradicts California Commercial Code § 2309. The original text did not specify a termination notice period. The added clause potentially violates the requirement of "reasonable notification" as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1: "Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable." Ten days may be deemed unreasonable, especially if the other party has made significant investments based on the contract's continuation, creating a legal contradiction.
